projects.title: Unit 3 Project — Ratios in Action
projects.desc: Capstone project: apply ratio reasoning to a real-world challenge.

projects.title: Unit Rate Duel
projects.desc: Compare ratios by finding and racing unit rates head-to-head.

projects.title: Ratio Rally
projects.desc: Compare ratios in a fast-paced ratio-reasoning game.

title: Compare Ratios
contentObjective: I can compare ratios by finding unit rates or using equivalent ratios.
languageObjective: I can explain my comparison using the words unit rate, equivalent ratio, compare, and common denominator.

turnAndTalk.question: Chef Reyes uses 3 tbsp cocoa for every 5 oz of milk, and Chef Tran uses 4 tbsp for every 7 oz. Can you tell whose cocoa is more chocolatey just by looking at the numbers? Why or why not?
turnAndTalk.kernel: You can't compare fairly until the ratios share the same amount of milk or you find cocoa per 1 oz.

stems.en: You ___ tell just by looking because the ___ amounts are different.
stems.es: ___ se puede saber solo mirando porque las cantidades de ___ son diferentes.

stems.en: To compare fairly we need the same ___ or the cocoa per ___.
stems.es: Para comparar de forma justa necesitamos la misma ___ o el cacao por ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Students recognize that 3:5 and 4:7 have different milk amounts, so a fair comparison needs equal milk (a common amount) or a unit rate (cocoa per 1 oz).
turnAndTalk.extend: Some students guess Chef Tran is more chocolatey because 4 is more than 3. Why is that reasoning not enough? Justify using the milk amounts.
extendStems: Looking only at the cocoa is not enough because ___.
More cocoa does not always mean more chocolatey since ___.

turnAndTalk.question: You scaled both recipes to 35 oz of milk: Chef Reyes became 21:35 and Chef Tran became 20:35. How does scaling to a common denominator let you compare the recipes fairly?
turnAndTalk.kernel: When both ratios share 35 oz of milk, you only have to compare the cocoa amounts.

stems.en: After scaling to 35 oz, Chef Reyes has ___ tbsp and Chef Tran has ___ tbsp.
stems.es: Después de escalar a 35 oz, Chef Reyes tiene ___ cucharadas y Chef Tran tiene ___ cucharadas.

stems.en: Now I can compare because the ___ is the same, so I just compare the ___.
stems.es: Ahora puedo comparar porque la ___ es igual, así que solo comparo el ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Students explain that equal milk (35 oz) makes the comparison fair, so 21 tbsp > 20 tbsp means Chef Reyes is more chocolatey.
turnAndTalk.extend: Find the unit rate (cocoa per 1 oz) for each chef and show it gives the same winner as the 35 oz scaling. Why must both methods agree?
extendStems: The unit rates are about ___ and ___ tbsp per oz, so ___ wins.

turnAndTalk.question: Two pizza shops have deals: Mario's sells 2 large pizzas for $18, and Sal's sells 3 for $25. How would you compare these to find the best price per pizza?
turnAndTalk.kernel: Find the unit rate (price per 1 pizza) for each shop, then compare those rates.

stems.en: Mario's costs ___ per pizza because 18 divided by 2 equals ___.
stems.es: Mario's cuesta ___ por pizza porque 18 entre 2 es ___.

stems.en: The better deal is ___ because its ___ is lower.
stems.es: La mejor oferta es ___ porque su ___ es más baja.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Students compute $9.00/pizza for Mario's and about $8.33/pizza for Sal's, then choose Sal's because the lower unit rate is the better deal.
turnAndTalk.extend: If you only need 2 pizzas, does the better unit rate always mean the better total price? Explain when buying the deal with the lower unit rate might not save money.
extendStems: A lower unit rate is the best total deal only when ___.
It might not save money if ___.

vocabulary.definition: A rate for just 1 of something, like cost for 1 item.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Una tasa para solo 1 de algo, como el precio de 1 artículo.
vocabulary.cloze: A rate that tells the amount for exactly one unit, like 5 miles per 1 hour, is a ___.

vocabulary.definition: Two ratios that mean the same thing.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Dos razones que significan lo mismo.

vocabulary.definition: To look at ratios and see which is bigger, smaller, or equal.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Mirar razones para ver cuál es mayor, menor o igual.
vocabulary.visual: 3:4 vs. 2:5 — which has more of the first ingredient per unit?
vocabulary.cloze: To decide which ratio is greater or if they are equal is to ___ them.

vocabulary.definition: To make a ratio as small as possible while keeping the same comparison.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Hacer una razón lo más pequeña posible sin cambiar la comparación.
vocabulary.cloze: Writing a ratio in its smallest equal form is to ___ it.

vocabulary.definition: A bottom number that two fractions can share so you can compare them.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Un número de abajo que dos fracciones pueden compartir para compararlas.
vocabulary.visual: To compare 3/5 and 4/7, use LCD 35: 21/35 vs 20/35
vocabulary.cloze: A denominator shared by two fractions so they can be compared is a ___.

launch.narrative: Chef Academy is holding a Flavor Challenge! Chef Reyes's hot cocoa recipe uses 3 tablespoons of cocoa for every 5 ounces of milk. Chef Tran's recipe uses 4 tablespoons of cocoa for every 7 ounces of milk. The students need to figure out whose hot cocoa is more chocolatey. How can they compare the two recipes fairly?
noticePrompts: What quantities are being compared in each recipe?
Can you tell just by looking which recipe has more cocoa per ounce?
What would make the comparison fair?
wonderPrompts: What if both chefs used the same amount of milk — then could we compare?
Is there a way to find how much cocoa each chef uses per ONE ounce of milk?
launch.contextImage: Two chefs side by side, each with their hot cocoa recipe card, measuring spoons, and mugs

conceptIntro.heading: How do we compare two ratios?
conceptIntro.intro: To compare ratios fairly, make one amount the same in both, or find the unit rate (the amount for just 1). Then you can see which ratio is bigger.
conceptIntro.keyIdea: Compare ratios fairly by making one part equal or by finding the unit rate.

iDo.title: Watch me
lines: Chef Reyes uses 3 tbsp cocoa for 5 oz milk (3:5). Chef Tran uses 4 tbsp for 7 oz milk (4:7).
I cannot compare yet because the milk amounts are different.
I scale both to 35 oz of milk. For Reyes: 5 x 7 = 35, so 3 x 7 = 21 tbsp, giving 21:35.
For Tran: 7 x 5 = 35, so 4 x 5 = 20 tbsp, giving 20:35.
Now the milk is equal, so I compare cocoa: 21 > 20, so Chef Reyes's cocoa is more chocolatey.

weDo.title: Let's try together
lines: Store A sells 2 pens for $6. Store B sells 3 pens for $12. Which is the better deal?
Find each unit rate, the price for 1 pen. Store A: 6 divided by 2 = $3 per pen.
Store B: 12 divided by 3 = $4 per pen. Store A is cheaper at $3 per pen.

youDo.title: Now it's your turn
lines: Next you will scale both cocoa recipes to 35 oz of milk and find each unit rate.
Then decide which chef's cocoa is more chocolatey.

explore.instructions: Build equivalent ratios for each chef's recipe so you can compare them fairly. Chef Reyes: 3 tbsp cocoa per 5 oz milk. Chef Tran: 4 tbsp cocoa per 7 oz milk.

optional.stem: Chef Academy's snack bar sells 3 mini quiches for $6 or 5 mini quiches for $8. Which is the better deal per quiche?
optional.explanation: 3 for $6 is $6 / 3 = $2.00 per quiche. 5 for $8 is $8 / 5 = $1.60 per quiche. $1.60 < $2.00, so 5 quiches for $8 is the better deal.

optional.stem: Two soup recipes use salt. Recipe A uses 4 tsp salt for 10 cups of broth. Recipe B uses 3 tsp salt for 8 cups of broth. Which soup is saltier per cup?
optional.explanation: Recipe A: 4 / 10 = 0.4 tsp per cup. Recipe B: 3 / 8 = 0.375 tsp per cup. Since 0.4 > 0.375, Recipe A is saltier per cup.

optional.stem: Chef Mara's lemon glaze uses 6 oz of lemon juice for 9 cupcakes. Chef Devi's uses 4 oz for 7 cupcakes. Whose glaze has more lemon per cupcake?
optional.explanation: Chef Mara: 6 / 9 = 0.666... oz per cupcake. Chef Devi: 4 / 7 = 0.571... oz per cupcake. Since 0.67 > 0.57, Chef Mara's glaze has more lemon per cupcake.

optional.label: Match each Chef Academy ratio comparison to the correct winner.

pairs.term: Smoothie A: 2 cups fruit for 6 cups water  vs.  Smoothie B: 3 cups fruit for 8 cups water — more fruity?
Bagels: 4 for $5  vs.  6 for $9 — better price per bagel?
Trail mix: 6 oz nuts to 10 oz oats  vs.  9 oz nuts to 15 oz oats — more nutty?
Dressing: 3 tbsp oil to 4 tbsp vinegar  vs.  5 tbsp oil to 8 tbsp vinegar — more oily?

optional.instructions: Each chef compared two recipes. Sort their conclusions into Correct or Incorrect.

items.text: Sauce 5:6 vs. 7:9 — chef says 5:6 is thicker (more tomato)
Punch 2 cups juice for 5 cups water vs. 3 for 8 — chef says the 2:5 punch is more juicy
Frosting 3:4 sugar-to-butter vs. 5:8 — chef says 3:4 is sweeter
Cocoa 3:7 vs. 2:5 — chef says 3:7 is weaker (less cocoa per oz)
Tea 4:5 leaves-to-water vs. 5:7 — chef says 5:7 is stronger

optional.title: Level 2 Extension — Chef Omar's Glaze Comparison

workedExample.label: Read the problem
workedExample.work: Chef Omar compares two glazes: 3 oz syrup for 8 oz water and 5 oz syrup for 12 oz water. Which is sweeter per ounce of water?

workedExample.label: Look at the syrup
workedExample.work: Glaze 1 has 3 oz syrup. Glaze 2 has 5 oz syrup.

workedExample.label: Compare the syrup amounts
workedExample.work: Since 5 > 3, Glaze 2 must have more syrup per ounce of water.

workedExample.label: Conclusion
workedExample.work: Glaze 2 is sweeter per ounce of water.

optional.correctWork: You cannot just compare the syrup amounts because the water amounts are different (8 oz vs 12 oz). Find the unit rate: Glaze 1 = 3 / 8 = 0.375 oz syrup per oz water; Glaze 2 = 5 / 12 = 0.416... oz syrup per oz water. Since 0.417 > 0.375, Glaze 2 is sweeter per ounce — but this must be proven with unit rates, not by comparing 5 > 3 alone.
hints: Look at Step 3. Chef Omar compared only the syrup (numerators) without making the water (the other term) equal.
Find the syrup per 1 oz of water for each glaze, then compare those unit rates.

approaching.stem: Chef A uses 2 cups of cheese for every 8 crackers. Chef B uses 3 cups of cheese for every 9 crackers. Who uses more cheese per cracker?
approaching.explanation: Chef A: 2÷8 = 0.25 cups per cracker. Chef B: 3÷9 ≈ 0.33 cups per cracker. Chef B uses more cheese per cracker.
Convert to the same denominator: 3:4 = 6:8. Since 6:8 > 5:8, the ratio 3:4 is greater.

approaching.stem: Which lemonade recipe is more lemony? Recipe A: 2 lemons for 6 cups of water. Recipe B: 3 lemons for 7 cups of water.
approaching.explanation: Recipe A: 2÷6 ≈ 0.33 lemons per cup. Recipe B: 3÷7 ≈ 0.43 lemons per cup. Since 0.43 > 0.33, Recipe B is more lemony.

approaching.stem: Which is the better deal: 4 apples for $3 or 6 apples for $5?
approaching.explanation: Deal 1: $3÷4 = $0.75 per apple. Deal 2: $5÷6 ≈ $0.83 per apple. $0.75 < $0.83, so 4 apples for $3 is the better deal.

approaching.instructions: Find the unit rate for each option to compare them.

onLevel.instructions: Match each scenario to the correct answer: Which ratio is greater?

onLevel.stem: Store A sells 3 mangoes for $6. Store B sells 5 mangoes for $9. Which store offers a better price per mango?
onLevel.explanation: Store A: $6÷3 = $2.00 per mango. Store B: $9÷5 = $1.80 per mango. Store B has the better (lower) price per mango.

onLevel.instructions: Two paint recipes both make green. Recipe A: 3 parts blue to 5 parts yellow. Recipe B: 4 parts blue to 7 parts yellow. Scale both to a common amount of yellow to compare which recipe is more blue.

onLevel.label: Place each unit rate on the number line to compare: A = $0.60/oz, B = $0.75/oz, C = $0.50/oz

extending.title: Find Kai's Mistake

workedExample.label: Read the problem
workedExample.work: Compare 3:5 and 4:7. Which ratio is greater?

extending.correctWork: Since 21 < 28, the ratio 4:7 (which equals 28:35) is greater, not 3:5.
hints: Look at Step 4. Kai correctly found 21:35 and 28:35 — but then drew the wrong conclusion.
The larger numerator (28) means that ratio is greater. Which original ratio gave 28:35?

extending.stem: Store A sells 5 notebooks for $12. Store B sells 8 notebooks for $20. Without calculating, a student says Store B must be cheaper because you get more notebooks. Is the student's reasoning valid? Find the unit rates to prove your answer.
extending.sampleAnswer: The student's reasoning is not valid — getting more items does not mean a lower price per item. Store A: $12 ÷ 5 = $2.40 per notebook. Store B: $20 ÷ 8 = $2.50 per notebook. Store A is actually cheaper per notebook, even though Store B offers more notebooks per purchase.

extending.instructions: Chef A uses a 2:3 ratio of oil to vinegar. Chef B uses a 3:5 ratio. If both chefs use 15 tablespoons of vinegar, which uses more oil? Use bar models to compare.
extending.label: Chef A: 2:3 → with 15 tbsp vinegar, oil = 10 tbsp. Chef B: 3:5 → with 15 tbsp vinegar, oil = 9 tbsp. Chef A uses more oil.

optionalActivity.name: Best Deal Taste Test
optionalActivity.intro: Step up to the Chef Academy tasting counter and judge which recipe or deal wins by comparing ratios fairly!

connect.scenario: Two pizza shops are running deals. Mario's Pizza sells 2 large pizzas for $18. Sal's Pizza sells 3 large pizzas for $25. A school needs to buy pizza for a class party and wants to get the best deal per pizza.
connect.prompt: This is like our ratio comparison work because ___ and ___ are related by ___.

exitTicket.stem: A bakery sells 4 muffins for $10 and another bakery sells 6 muffins for $14. Which bakery has the lower price per muffin?
exitTicket.explanation: First bakery: $10÷4 = $2.50 per muffin. Second bakery: $14÷6 ≈ $2.33 per muffin. The second bakery has the lower price.
